seo.title: Cybersecurity Content Generation AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for content generation AI agents in cybersecurity. Covers NIST Cybersecurity Framework, SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001.

content.intro: Deploying AI agents for cybersecurity content generation introduces unique risks where LLM outputs could inadvertently expose threat intelligence, misrepresent CVE severity, or generate advisories that mislead security teams into harmful response actions. This checklist ensures your AI content pipeline meets NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, and ISO 27001 requirements while maintaining editorial integrity and preventing adversarial exploitation of AI-generated outputs. Given the critical sensitivity of cybersecurity content, every item here directly maps to a failure mode with real-world breach or outage consequences.

sections.heading: Data Isolation & Threat Intelligence Containment
sections.description: Prevent threat intelligence, IOCs, and classified adversary TTPs from leaking through LLM API calls, training pipelines, or generated content outputs.

items.label: Sanitize LLM API Inputs of Live Threat Data
items.description: Implement a pre-processing filter that strips active IOCs (IP addresses, hashes, domains), classified threat actor TTPs, and unreleased vulnerability details before sending any prompt to external LLM APIs. Maintain an allowlist of shareable data categories.

items.label: Enforce Data Residency for Sensitive Prompt Logs
items.description: Configure LLM API providers to disable prompt logging and model training on your inputs, and verify this via contractual DPA agreements. For on-premise deployments, restrict prompt logs to SOC 2-compliant storage with access controls audited quarterly.

items.label: Classify Content Requests by Sensitivity Tier
items.description: Tag all content generation requests as Public, Internal, Confidential, or Restricted before routing to the LLM. Restricted and Confidential requests must route to air-gapped or self-hosted models only, never to third-party APIs.

items.label: Implement Output Scanning for Accidental Intelligence Disclosure
items.description: Deploy a post-generation scanner that checks AI outputs for patterns matching internal threat intelligence formats (e.g., STIX/TAXII objects, internal CVE tracking IDs, internal network ranges) before content is published or shared.

items.label: Audit LLM Vendor Sub-processors Against ISO 27001 Annex A
items.description: Obtain and review the LLM vendor's sub-processor list annually. Verify each sub-processor holds ISO 27001 certification or equivalent, and document this in your vendor risk register as required by ISO 27001 Clause 8.1.

items.label: Establish Incident Response Playbook for AI Data Leakage
items.description: Create a specific IR playbook for the scenario where threat intelligence is confirmed to have been included in an LLM API call. Define containment steps, notification timelines per CISA directives, and post-incident review requirements.

sections.heading: Content Accuracy & CVE Verification Controls
sections.description: Ensure all AI-generated security advisories, vulnerability write-ups, and threat reports are verified against authoritative sources before publication to prevent misinformation that could trigger false-positive incident responses.

items.label: Integrate Real-Time CVE Database Verification
items.description: Connect the content generation pipeline to NVD (National Vulnerability Database) and MITRE CVE APIs. Every AI-generated advisory referencing a CVE ID must programmatically verify the CVE exists, confirm CVSS score accuracy, and flag discrepancies for human review before publication.

items.label: Require Human Editorial Approval for All Security Advisories
items.description: Enforce a mandatory human-in-the-loop review gate for any AI-generated content that includes vulnerability severity ratings, recommended remediation actions, or incident response guidance. No such content may be auto-published without a named approver logged in the audit trail.

items.label: Implement Hallucination Detection for Technical Claims
items.description: Deploy a secondary LLM or rule-based checker specifically trained to identify fabricated CVE IDs, non-existent vendor patches, and incorrect CVSS vectors in generated content. Flag confidence scores below 0.85 for mandatory human review.

items.label: Cross-Reference CISA KEV Catalog for Exploitability Claims
items.description: Automatically query the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog when AI content makes claims about active exploitation. Ensure generated content accurately reflects KEV status and does not understate exploitation risk for listed vulnerabilities.

items.label: Version-Control All Published AI-Generated Security Content
items.description: Store every published AI-generated advisory in a version-controlled repository with timestamps, approver identity, and the specific LLM model version used. This enables rapid retraction and correction if a factual error is discovered post-publication.

items.label: Establish Maximum Staleness Thresholds for Referenced Threat Data
items.description: Define and enforce a policy that AI-generated content may not reference threat intelligence older than 90 days without explicit disclosure. Automated checks should flag content referencing deprecated CVE statuses or outdated CVSS scores.

items.label: Validate Remediation Guidance Against Vendor Security Bulletins
items.description: Before publishing AI-generated remediation steps, programmatically check the relevant vendor's official security bulletin (e.g., Microsoft MSRC, Cisco PSIRT) to confirm patch availability and accuracy of recommended actions.

sections.heading: Brand Voice, Tone & Editorial Compliance
sections.description: Enforce consistent brand voice, responsible disclosure standards, and editorial guidelines to prevent AI-generated content from creating legal liability, reputational damage, or audience confusion in the cybersecurity domain.

items.label: Define and Encode Cybersecurity Brand Voice Parameters
items.description: Document explicit brand voice rules for cybersecurity content (e.g., authoritative but not alarmist, technical but accessible, neutral on vendor attribution) and encode these as system prompt constraints and fine-tuning guidelines for the LLM.

items.label: Implement Responsible Disclosure Language Enforcement
items.description: Create a content policy filter that detects and blocks AI-generated content that names unpatched vulnerabilities with full exploitation details, attributes attacks to specific threat actors without verified evidence, or includes proof-of-concept exploit code.

items.label: Enforce Tone Consistency Scoring Across Content Types
items.description: Deploy an automated tone analysis tool that scores each AI-generated piece against defined tone parameters (urgency level, technical depth, call-to-action strength) and flags deviations greater than 20% from baseline for editorial review.

items.label: Validate SEO Metadata Does Not Expose Internal Taxonomy
items.description: Audit AI-generated SEO tags, meta descriptions, and URL slugs to ensure they do not inadvertently expose internal threat classification taxonomies, project codenames, or client-specific terminology that could reveal intelligence to adversaries.

items.label: Run Originality Checks Against Published Threat Intelligence Reports
items.description: Submit all AI-generated content through a plagiarism and originality checker calibrated against major threat intelligence publications (Mandiant, CrowdStrike, Recorded Future) to prevent unintentional reproduction of proprietary research.

items.label: Maintain an Approved Cybersecurity Terminology Glossary
items.description: Provide the LLM with a curated, approved glossary of cybersecurity terms with preferred definitions and usage examples. Automated checks should flag use of deprecated terminology (e.g., 'hacker' in ambiguous contexts) or inconsistent acronym expansion.

items.label: Require Legal Review Trigger for Attribution and Liability Claims
items.description: Implement a keyword trigger system that automatically routes AI-generated content containing threat actor attribution, legal liability language, or regulatory compliance claims to a legal reviewer before publication.

sections.heading: Governance, Audit Trails & Regulatory Compliance
sections.description: Establish the audit infrastructure, access controls, and compliance documentation required to satisfy NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, and ISO 27001 requirements for AI-assisted content generation in a critical cybersecurity context.

items.label: Maintain Immutable Audit Logs for All AI Content Generation Events
items.description: Log every content generation request with timestamp, user identity, input prompt hash (not full prompt if sensitive), model version, output hash, and approval chain. Store logs in a WORM (Write Once Read Many) compliant system for minimum 12 months per SOC 2 requirements.

items.label: Implement Role-Based Access Control for Content Pipeline
items.description: Define and enforce RBAC with least-privilege principles: content requestors cannot approve their own content, security reviewers must have verified CVE database access, and admin roles require MFA. Map roles to ISO 27001 Annex A.9 access control requirements.

items.label: Conduct Quarterly AI Content Risk Assessments
items.description: Perform structured risk assessments every quarter evaluating new LLM capabilities, emerging prompt injection techniques targeting cybersecurity content, and changes in the threat landscape that could affect content accuracy requirements. Document findings per NIST CSF Identify function.

items.label: Map AI Content Controls to NIST CSF Functions
items.description: Create and maintain a control mapping document that explicitly links each content generation safeguard to NIST CSF functions (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover). This document must be reviewed and updated with each major LLM upgrade or pipeline change.

items.label: Include AI Content Pipeline in Annual SOC 2 Type II Audit Scope
items.description: Formally include the AI content generation system in your SOC 2 Type II audit scope under the Availability, Confidentiality, and Processing Integrity trust service criteria. Provide auditors with evidence of automated controls, human review gates, and exception handling.

items.label: Establish Model Change Management Process
items.description: Define a formal change management process for LLM model updates that includes regression testing of brand voice compliance, re-validation of CVE verification integrations, and security review of new model capabilities before deployment to production content pipelines.

items.label: Document AI Content Limitations in Published Disclaimers
items.description: Add standardized disclaimers to all AI-assisted cybersecurity content disclosing AI involvement, verification date of referenced CVEs, and a link to report inaccuracies. This satisfies transparency requirements under emerging AI governance frameworks and reduces liability.

items.label: Test Prompt Injection Defenses Monthly
items.description: Conduct monthly red team exercises specifically targeting the content generation pipeline with prompt injection attacks designed to extract threat intelligence, bypass editorial controls, or generate irresponsible disclosure content. Document results and remediation in your risk register.

regulatoryNotes.heading: Regulatory Considerations
notes: NIST CSF Govern Function (v2.0): AI content generation systems must be included in your organizational cybersecurity governance scope. The new Govern function requires documented policies for AI tool usage, defined accountability for AI-generated content accuracy, and integration of AI risk into enterprise risk management processes.

SOC 2 Type II Processing Integrity: Automated AI content pipelines must demonstrate that outputs are complete, valid, accurate, timely, and authorized. This requires documented evidence of CVE verification integrations, human approval gates, and exception handling procedures that auditors can test over the audit period.

ISO 27001:2022 Clause 6.1.2 Information Security Risk Assessment: The use of external LLM APIs for cybersecurity content generation must be formally assessed as an information security risk, with documented treatment decisions. Annex A.5.23 (Information security for use of cloud services) specifically applies to LLM API consumption.

CISA AI Cybersecurity Guidelines: CISA's guidelines on AI use in critical infrastructure require that AI-generated security advisories and threat content undergo human verification before influencing defensive actions. Organizations must maintain the ability to rapidly retract AI-generated content and notify affected parties if inaccurate advisories are discovered to have influenced security decisions.

cta.heading: Automate Your Cybersecurity Agent Compliance
cta.description: Deploy Vex to continuously monitor your AI content pipeline against NIST CSF, SOC 2, and ISO 27001 controls—automatically flagging CVE inaccuracies, data leakage risks, and editorial violations before they reach publication.
